Research

Projects
The Alpha Project

Biological systems are highly determined, in the sense that a great deal of their future behavior depends upon their current state. For individual cells, the decision to adopt different courses of behavior depends on information input from their genomes, from their internal machinery, and from the environment outside the cell.

Molecular biology as it developed during the 20th century has resulted in unprecedented understanding of the information in the genome, in the workings of the internal machinery, and in the relevant signals in the external environment. However, this level of understanding is not as deep as a basic scientist would wish. Nor is it as deep as medical, pharmaceutical, and agricultural researchers, physicians and farmers who wish to apply this understanding would wish, and the public which supports these activities, should demand.

Biologists cannot translate their hard-won knowledge of how cells decide to divide into accurate predictions of how specific tumor cells will respond to specific anticancer drugs. Biologists can sequence an organisms' genome, but they cannot from that sequence predict the envelope of possibilities that that genome encodes.

This defines one of the great challenges of post-genome-sequence molecular biology: to learn how ensembles of components, proteins encoded by the genome and the other molecules manufactured by those proteins, work together to create complex biological outcomes. The activities at MSI are focused on obtaining that understanding.

Ongoing Research Activities

The Alpha Project

The flagship research activity at MSI is the Alpha Project. The Alpha Project is an attempt to understand the quantitative behavior of the response of Saccharomyces cerevisiae to mating pheromone. The network of proteins that governs this response is probably the best understood genetic system in eukaryotes, and is among the best understood regulatory network in any organism. However, as mentioned above, we cannot now predict the quantitative behavior of this pathway in response to defined perturbations. The Alpha Project seeks to rectify that.

The main tasks to be accomplished during the course of the project are: a) the creation and use of new experimental methods to measure numbers of different molecules in the pathway and rates at which they change into one another and b) creation of quantitative models that make use of those measurements to predict future behavior.

Some of the methods being developed are to gain means of making the required measurements from large numbers of individual single cells. This work involves significant intracellular protein engineering and, increasingly, attention to the microscopic and other optical methods needed to measure fluorescent signals from the cells.

Other methods under development are to gain means of making the measurements from populations of cells. These range from careful use and calibration of standard wet-lab techniques such as RT-PCR (Real Time/ Reverse Transcription Polymerase chain reaction) all the way to attempts to quantify the amounts and rates of protein phosphorylation using FT-ICR Protein Mass Spec (Fourier Transform Ion Cyclotron Resonance Protein Mass Spectrometry).

Still other methods under development are computational. These include ways to generate and solve sets of differential equations describing reactions in the pathway. They also include ways to represent the qualitative biological knowledge that is the starting point for the quantitative representations of the system. They also include ways to derive further information from the existing models.

Activities on the project are centered at MSI, but there is significant effort in labs at other sites, including UC Berkeley, MIT, Caltech, and Pacific Northwest National Laboratory. Work on the project is under the aegis of the "Center for Genomic Experimentation and Computation", which is funded under the Centers of Excellence program of the NIH National Human Genome Research Institute. Initial work for the computational aspects of the project received significant support from the DARPA Information Processing Technology Office.

Regulatory Pathway Components and Connection

External to the Alpha Project, there is significant work at MSI in improving methods to identify proteins and protein-protein interactions that govern regulatory processes. These include continuing efforts to develop two-hybrid methodology, to perfect schemes for severing individual pathway connections by mutagenesis, and to inactivate individual components and connections by peptide aptamer reagents. These methods are being applied to explore the connection(s) between the systems in higher cells that govern cell proliferation and cell death, and, in one case, to explore an idea that might link cell division and cell senescence. This work is supported by the National Human Genome Research Institute and has been supported in the past by the National Institute for General Medical Science.

Another significant effort is to use peptide aptamers to identify regulatory proteins and pathway connections governing important developmental decisions in mammalian stem cells. This work is conducted in collaboration with James Thompson at the University of Wisconsin, Madison and is supported by the Ellison Medical Foundation.

Whole Genome Evolution and Fitness

It now seems apparent that the pace of evolution is likely to be influenced by the external environment. Both the rate of point mutation and the amount of phenotypic variation caused by otherwise cryptic mutations in signal transduction pathways are now thought to be under the control of the external environment whose effects are mediated by known gene products. At MSI, there is significant work to characterize the nature and the mechanism governing environmentally induced genome rearrangements. As compared with the other two mechanisms, one important consequence of genome rearrangements is that they can trigger the formation of new species. This work is supported by the National Science Foundation.
